turns.content: I have a working solution using rand::thread_rng for some token generation, but I keep reading about OsRng and getrandom. Is there a better way to handle this?

You're right to look into those options because rand::thread_rng relies on the OS RNG which is fine for general use but not cryptographic security. The real issue is that you might be assuming all random functions are secure by default, when OsRng and getrandom serve different purposes depending on whether you need true randomness or just pseudo-randomness.
Oh I see, so if I'm generating tokens or nonces for security, I should probably use something else instead of the standard RNG?

Exactly. For cryptographic purposes you need to use a CSPRNG like OsRng or getrandom crate which are designed specifically for that purpose and ensure your random numbers meet security requirements unlike the basic rand implementations.
I just generated a token using rand::thread_rng and it seems to work for my app, but I've heard warnings about this approach. Is there a better way to get truly secure randomness in Rust?

For cryptographic purposes you should avoid the standard random number generator and instead use OsRng from the crypto-rand crate or the getrandom crate which accesses /dev/urandom on Unix systems. Think of it like trying to make a fair coin by shaking your hand versus using a dedicated machine designed for randomness; your manual implementation introduces bias while these crates provide the statistical properties required for security.
I see, but I'm confused about the difference between OsRng and getrandom since they seem to do similar things. Can you explain when each should be used?

OsRng is part of the rand ecosystem and wraps system sources while getrandom is specifically designed for cryptographic operations with better performance guarantees; use OsRng for general randomness in simulations but switch to getrandom whenever you need cryptographic strength like generating passwords or session tokens.
